core_claim: In 2024 a finance worker at Arup wired about 25 million dollars after a video call with a deepfaked CFO and fake colleagues, which proves that recognizing a face or voice on a call is not an authorization control.

caption:
The next urgent request from your CFO might be a deepfake, and it might be on video.

In 2024, the engineering firm Arup lost about $25 million after a finance worker in its Hong Kong office joined a video call with people he recognized as the CFO and colleagues. Every one of them was a deepfake. He made fifteen transfers before anyone realized. Arup said none of its internal systems were breached, and its own CIO called it technology-enhanced social engineering. CNN, the South China Morning Post, and Fortune all reported the same figures.

The lesson is not that video calls are dangerous. It is that recognizing a face or a voice was never a strong authorization control, and AI just made that obvious. The cruel part of this case is that the verification step itself, the live call with familiar faces, was the forgery. Real-time social proof overrode the employee's first, correct suspicion.

The FBI has warned that criminals now generate real-time video of executives and authority figures, and it recommends a pre-agreed code word to confirm identity.

Defender move: require out-of-band verification for money movement, every time. Call back a known, saved number, not one from the request, and require dual approval with a built-in delay. Build it so no one can skip it because the boss was on the call.

Could a convincing video call trigger a wire transfer at your company today?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Could a convincing video call trigger a wire transfer at your company today, or would a callback stop it?
alt_text: Title card: They did a video call to verify the request, and the video call was the scam. In 2024 a finance worker wired about 25 million dollars after a deepfake call with his CFO. A boardroom table is lined with faceless suited mannequins.

At the engineering firm Arup, a finance worker joined a video call with people he recognized, then made fifteen transfers. Every person on that call was a deepfake, and none of the company's systems were breached. Framed portraits tip to reveal hollow cardboard backs.

A request can now look and sound like someone your team trusts, in real time, on a live call. Recognizing a face was never a strong control, and AI just made that obvious. A wax bust of a businessman begins to slump and melt while holding its features.

Several familiar faces agreeing on one call is social proof, and it overrode the employee's first, correct suspicion. The verification step itself was the forgery. A row of identical figurines topples in sequence after an unseen hand tips the first.

Approval flows that trust a recognized person, not a verified process, fail the moment the person can be faked. If a live call can waive your controls, you do not really have controls. A hand slides a stack of banknotes toward an open briefcase.

Verify money movement out-of-band: call back a known, saved number, and require dual approval with a delay. Build it so no one can skip it because the boss was on the call. An open ledger shows two ink stamps and a hand poised over a third.

A face on a live call is not authorization. Trust the verified process, not the person you recognize. An ornate picture frame stands in deep shadow with its canvas cut clean out, a thin rim of light along the inner edge.
Could a convincing video call trigger a wire transfer at your company today? Save this for your next finance-controls review. A single unsigned cheque lies alone in vast dark negative space.

sources.source: CNN: British engineering firm Arup loses $25M in deepfake scam
sources.supports: Arup finance worker in Hong Kong duped by a video call with a deepfaked CFO and colleagues; ~HK$200M (~US$25.6M) lost over ~15 transfers; early 2024.

sources.source: South China Morning Post: Arup confirmed victim of HK$200M deepfake scam
sources.supports: Independent confirmation: digital version of the CFO used on a video call; 15 transactions to 5 accounts; no internal systems breached.

sources.source: Fortune: Arup deepfake fraud, Hong Kong, $25 million
sources.supports: Independent confirmation of the ~$25M loss and deepfake video-conference method.

sources.source: FBI IC3 Public Service Announcement, December 2024
sources.supports: Criminals generate real-time video of executives and authority figures; FBI recommends a pre-agreed secret word to verify identity.

narration.text: They did a video call to verify the request, and the video call itself was the scam.
At the engineering firm Arup in 2024, a finance worker joined a call with his CFO and colleagues and wired about 25 million dollars over fifteen transfers. Every face on that call was a deepfake.
So verify money movement out of band. Call back a known, saved number, and require dual approval with a built-in delay.
A face on a live call is not authorization. Trust the verified process, not the person you recognize.
Could a convincing video call trigger a wire transfer at your company today? Tell me below.
